Best Picture.Bugonia: Here’s the pitch: weird, stylish, and just chaotic enough that voting for it makes people feel adventurous.
Best Picture.F1: This is the big, slick, movie-star crowd-pleaser, which matters because sometimes the Academy also enjoys having fun.
Best Picture.Frankenstein: Classic monster story plus Guillermo del Toro equals prestige with bolts in its neck, which is a very real Oscar lane.
Best Picture.Hamnet: Period drama, grief, literary pedigree, gorgeous filmmaking, this is premium Oscar catnip with excellent posture.
Best Picture.Marty Supreme: The case here is pure juice: if voters want to reward the movie that feels the most alive, this is a very live wire pick.
Best Picture.One Battle After Another: This is the big prestige machine in the race, the kind of movie people pick when they want the smart answer and the safe one.
Best Picture.The Secret Agent: This is the serious international contender, the pick for voters who want something classy, tense, and a little less obvious.
Best Picture.Sentimental Value: Think beautifully acted family drama with emotional shrapnel everywhere, which is exactly the sort of thing Oscar voters eat up.
Best Picture.Sinners: This is the passion pick, the cool pick, and maybe the winner if the Academy wants its biggest vote to feel exciting.
Best Picture.Train Dreams: Quiet, soulful, and very much in the serious-movie lane, this is the one for people who want beauty over bombast.

Best Director.Chloé Zhao — Hamnet: The case for Zhao is elegant misery done beautifully, which is a sentence Oscar voters have historically enjoyed.
Best Director.Josh Safdie — Marty Supreme: If voters want to reward pure energy and a director who made chaos feel thrilling, this is the fun swing.
Best Director.Paul Thomas Anderson — One Battle After: This is the classic 'it might finally be his time' pick, which is a powerful little Oscar engine.
Best Director.Joachim Trier — Sentimental Value: The appeal here is emotional precision, like if family pain were directed with a laser pointer and taste.
Best Director.Ryan Coogler — Sinners: If voters want to crown the filmmaker behind the movie people are most fired up about, Coogler is the loud, good answer.

Best Actor.Timothée Chalamet — Marty Supreme: Big star, flashy role, lots of charisma, this is the pick if you think the Academy wants the performance with the most electricity.
Best Actor.Leonardo DiCaprio — One Battle After Ano: Leo is always lurking, and if this movie has a huge night, he is the classic sweep-along-with-the-frontrunner pick.
Best Actor.Ethan Hawke — Blue Moon: This is the respected-actor pick, the kind of performance industry people rally behind because they know exactly how hard it is.
Best Actor.Michael B. Jordan — Sinners: Jordan has the momentum and the movie people are obsessed with, which is a very dangerous combination on Oscar night.
Best Actor.Wagner Moura — The Secret Agent: This is the smart spoiler pick, serious, intense, and just left-of-center enough to feel cool without trying too hard.

Best Actress.Jessie Buckley — Hamnet: Buckley is the emotionally devastating prestige pick, which is usually a pretty healthy place to be in this category.
Best Actress.Rose Byrne — If I Had Legs I'd Kick You: The title comes in hot, and so does the performance, with exactly the kind of fearless chaos that gets insider buzz moving.
Best Actress.Kate Hudson — Song Sung Blue: This is the comeback-story play, where a familiar star shows up with something richer than expected and people go wait, really?
Best Actress.Renate Reinsve — Sentimental Value: The case here is subtle heartbreak done at a very high level, which can be catnip for voters who like precision over fireworks.
Best Actress.Emma Stone — Bugonia: Stone in a weird, ambitious movie is never a bad bet, especially when the role lets her do something bold and a little unhinged.

Best Supporting Actor.Benicio del Toro — One Battle After Anot: This is the cool, dangerous supporting turn that can win when a legend strolls in and owns the room.
Best Supporting Actor.Jacob Elordi — Frankenstein: Handsome monster with real acting ambition is exactly the kind of transformation arc the Academy loves to reward.
Best Supporting Actor.Delroy Lindo — Sinners: The appeal here is pure respect, as in people love him, the movie is hot, and a win would feel both inspired and overdue.
Best Supporting Actor.Sean Penn — One Battle After Another: Penn is the blunt-force prestige option, the kind of pick voters make when they want intensity with no garnish.
Best Supporting Actor.Stellan Skarsgård — Sentimental Value: This is the quiet gravitas pick, for anyone who likes their emotional damage delivered by someone who sounds like truth itself.

Best Supporting Actress.Elle Fanning — Sentimental Value: The case for Fanning is luminous, elegant support work, the kind that sneaks up on you and then wrecks you politely.
Best Supporting Actress.Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas — Sentimental Va: This is the discovery pick, the one for voters who like backing a performance that feels fresh instead of pre-sold.
Best Supporting Actress.Amy Madigan — Weapons: Veteran actor, strong buzz, scene-stealer energy, this is exactly how supporting races get interesting in a hurry.
Best Supporting Actress.Wunmi Mosaku — Sinners: If Sinners has a big night, Mosaku is the kind of nominee who comes riding in on that wave with real force.
Best Supporting Actress.Teyana Taylor — One Battle After Another: This is the high-voltage spoiler, a pick for voters who want star power, presence, and a performance with some snap to it.

Best Animated Feature.Arco: This is the artsier animated pick, the one for voters who want to reward beauty, feeling, and a little tastefulness.
Best Animated Feature.Elio: Pixar is Pixar, which means even when it is not the loudest movie in the room, it is still very much in the room.
Best Animated Feature.KPop Demon Hunters: This is the chaos-pop juggernaut, and if the Academy wants the fun answer, the internet is already screaming it for them.
Best Animated Feature.Little Amélie or the Character of Rain: The pitch here is delicate, poetic, and international, which is often how animation voters prove they have feelings.
Best Animated Feature.Zootopia 2: Big studio sequel, broad appeal, known brand, this is the polished mainstream pick if voters want the least complicated answer.

Best Adapted Screenplay.Bugonia — Will Tracy: The case here is smart, strange writing that feels sharp enough to leave a mark, which screenplay voters tend to notice.
Best Adapted Screenplay.Frankenstein — Guillermo del Toro: Taking a story everybody knows and making it feel rich, tragic, and fresh again is exactly the kind of trick this category rewards.
Best Adapted Screenplay.Hamnet — Chloé Zhao & Maggie O'Farrell: Literary adaptation, emotional heft, and prestige built into the walls, this is a very Academy-friendly screenplay package.
Best Adapted Screenplay.One Battle After Another — Paul Thomas A: This is the 'give PTA the writing Oscar already' pick, which has been floating around the room for a while now.
Best Adapted Screenplay.Train Dreams — Clint Bentley & Greg Kwed: This is the quiet, soulful adaptation choice, the one for voters who like their writing elegant rather than flashy.

Best Original Screenplay.Blue Moon — Robert Kaplow: This is the writerly insider pick, the kind of nominee people rally around when they want to reward craft over noise.
Best Original Screenplay.It Was Just an Accident — Jafar Panahi: Urgent, serious, and globally admired, this is the pick for voters who want their ballot to feel like it means something.
Best Original Screenplay.Marty Supreme — Ronald Bronstein & Josh: If voters want originality with a pulse and maybe a little sweat on it, this is the screenplay with the most juice.
Best Original Screenplay.Sentimental Value — Eskil Vogt & Joachim: The case here is emotional intelligence done with surgical precision, which is catnip for this category.
Best Original Screenplay.Sinners — Ryan Coogler: This is the strong all-around contender with enough story, momentum, and respect to win on more than just vibes.

Best Original Score.Bugonia — Jerskin Fendrix: This is the oddball score pick, the one for voters who like music that feels a little unruly in a good way.
Best Original Score.Frankenstein — Alexandre Desplat: Desplat doing gothic grandeur is a pretty easy sell, like handing the Academy a velvet cape and saying enjoy.
Best Original Score.Hamnet — Max Richter: If you want a score that sounds like beautiful sadness drifting through candlelight, this is the classy choice.
Best Original Score.One Battle After Another — Jonny Greenwo: Greenwood plus PTA is basically its own Oscar dialect at this point, and voters definitely understand the accent.
Best Original Score.Sinners — Ludwig Goransson: This is the big momentum pick, helped by the fact that the movie itself feels like a major event.

Best Original Song.\"Dear Me\" — Diane Warren: Relentless: Diane Warren is basically her own annual Oscar subplot, so this is the pick if you believe the story finally turns.
Best Original Song.\"Golden\" — KPop Demon Hunters: Big hook, big energy, big crowd appeal, this is the song pick if you want the room to actually know the tune.
Best Original Song.\"I Lied to You\" — Sinners: A song attached to a heavyweight contender is always dangerous, especially when the movie around it has real heat.
Best Original Song.\"Sweet Dreams of Joy\" — Viva Verdi!: This is the sentimental swing, the kind of song vote that says yes, I would like my Oscar choices to feel romantic.
Best Original Song.\"Train Dreams\" — Train Dreams: Title song from a serious movie is the exact sort of thing that can feel quietly inevitable in this category.

Best Cinematography.Frankenstein: This category is basically who made the movie look the most amazing, and Frankenstein has that rich, gothic, expensive gloom working hard for it.
Best Cinematography.Marty Supreme: If you like camera work that feels alive and buzzing, this is the stylish adrenaline pick.
Best Cinematography.One Battle After Another: Big scope and major visual confidence make this the smart pick if you want something grand and obviously award-ready.
Best Cinematography.Sinners: This is the cool-looking movie pick, the one for voters who want the winner to feel modern, bold, and maybe a little historic.
Best Cinematography.Train Dreams: The pitch here is pure visual beauty, the kind of photography that makes people whisper this is cinema and mean it.

Best Film Editing.F1: Editing is basically rhythm and momentum, and a racing movie has a very easy case when it keeps your pulse doing little backflips.
Best Film Editing.Marty Supreme: If voters like editing that feels frantic on purpose and somehow still in control, this is the chaos-with-seatbelts option.
Best Film Editing.One Battle After Another: Big contender plus sharp pacing is a very classic editing winner recipe, and this one knows the formula.
Best Film Editing.Sentimental Value: This is the quieter editing play, where the real skill is emotional timing instead of just making things go fast.
Best Film Editing.Sinners: If the Academy wants to reward the movie that feels the most locked in, Sinners has a very real case here too.

Best Production Design.Frankenstein: This category is basically best movie world, and Frankenstein looks like it built a very expensive nightmare from scratch.
Best Production Design.Hamnet: If voters want gorgeous period detail and rooms that look like they hold actual history, this is the elegant pick.
Best Production Design.Marty Supreme: The case here is style, texture, and a world with personality, not just pretty wallpaper and some chairs.
Best Production Design.One Battle After Another: Big canvas, lots of moving parts, and the kind of fully built-out world that makes a movie feel important.
Best Production Design.Sinners: This is the atmosphere pick, for voters who care about whether a movie world feels cool, specific, and lived in.

Best Costume Design.Avatar: Fire and Ash: This is the giant swing pick, where the costumes are doing a lot and probably deserve a nap afterward.
Best Costume Design.Frankenstein: Dark, dramatic, prestige coats and gowns are very much in this category’s wheelhouse, and Frankenstein knows it.
Best Costume Design.Hamnet: This is the refined period-fashion pick, the one for voters who want beauty, detail, and old-world sadness stitched together.
Best Costume Design.Marty Supreme: If style and attitude matter more than historical embroidery, this is the fun spoiler.
Best Costume Design.Sinners: The appeal here is character-driven cool, with costumes that feel like part of the movie’s identity instead of just fabric.

Best Makeup and Hairstyling.Frankenstein: This category loves visible transformation, and building a gorgeous, tragic monster is about as visible as it gets.
Best Makeup and Hairstyling.Kokuho: The pitch here is transformation work so strong it makes you do the classic Oscar thing of whispering wow, that is makeup.
Best Makeup and Hairstyling.Sinners: If voters want bold looks that also feel story-driven, Sinners is the stylish answer on the board.
Best Makeup and Hairstyling.The Smashing Machine: Physical transformation is catnip in this category, especially when the work helps sell a whole new person on screen.
Best Makeup and Hairstyling.The Ugly Stepsister: Grotesque fairy-tale makeover chaos is exactly the kind of thing this category notices from across the room.

Best Sound.F1: This category is basically who made the movie sound the most immersive, and F1 has the easiest pitch in the world: race cars, baby.
Best Sound.Frankenstein: Creature sounds, atmosphere, dread, thunder, this is the pick if you want your Oscar choice to sound deliciously haunted.
Best Sound.One Battle After Another: Big scale and full-on immersion make this a strong sound play for voters who want the most cinematic option.
Best Sound.Sinners: If the Academy goes all in on Sinners, this is one of those craft categories it could easily gobble up along the way.
Best Sound.Sirāt: The appeal here is that the sound is part of the experience, not just background seasoning, which can matter a lot in this category.

Best Visual Effects.Avatar: Fire and Ash: This category is basically movie magic, and Avatar is the giant obvious boss fight everyone else has to survive.
Best Visual Effects.F1: If voters want to reward effects that disappear into realism instead of screaming look at me, F1 is the slick alternative.
Best Visual Effects.Jurassic World Rebirth: Dinosaurs remain extremely Oscar-coded in visual effects, even when the franchise itself is running on pure fossil fuel.
Best Visual Effects.The Lost Bus: The case here is invisible effects, meaning the trick is making danger feel terrifyingly real without showing off about it.
Best Visual Effects.Sinners: This is the stylish upset pick if voters decide the effects that support a whole vibe are cooler than the loudest spectacle.

Best Casting.Hamnet — Nina Gold: This category is basically who put together the perfect lineup of actors, and Nina Gold doing prestige drama is a very serious threat.
Best Casting.Marty Supreme — Jennifer Venditti: If voters want a cast that feels fresh, specific, and just a little dangerous, this is the cool pick.
Best Casting.One Battle After Another — Cassandra Kul: Big ensemble, big names, big balancing act, this is the straightforward pick if you think assembling the whole machine should count.
Best Casting.The Secret Agent — Gabriel Domingues: The pitch here is precision, finding exactly the right faces and energy for a serious, less flashy contender.
Best Casting.Sinners — Francine Maisler: This is the strongest buzz play in a brand-new category, helped by the fact that the whole ensemble clearly came to work.
